caveats: a: The BLS table covers employers of all sizes and does not separate small risk-bearing carriers from large national plans.
a: Occupation shares are not task-level AI exposure and include work already performed by rules engines, portals, and electronic claims systems.
a: Commercial group, individual, Medicare, Medicaid, supplemental, dental, and vision products have different labor and clinical-review intensity.
rho: The 92% statistic combines current use with plans and exploration and is not an implementation or realized-savings rate.
rho: NAIC states that AI generally supports rather than fully replaces underwriters, claims professionals, agents, and service representatives.
rho: Prior authorization and claims models face especially high clinical, fairness, documentation, and appeal constraints.
e: No source measures default-lens eligibility among the frozen LMM-band firms.
e: The 41-firm anchor uses a general-insurance margin bridge that may not fit health carriers with high medical-loss ratios, risk adjustment, and government-program revenue.
e: Legal entities, plans, insurance groups, regional subsidiaries, and provider-sponsored arrangements are not interchangeable transaction units.
s5: Gallup measures cross-industry intentions rather than completed health-carrier transfers.
s5: The NAIC filer count includes legal entities and organizational forms outside the LMM control lens.
s5: No cited source supplies an industry denominator of completed qualifying control deals.
q: Neither CMS nor NAIC measures five-year retention of implemented AI benefit at LMM carriers.
q: MLR rules differ by market, and administrative savings do not mechanically create a rebate if the claims-and-quality share remains above the threshold.
q: Commercial, Medicare, Medicaid, supplemental, dental, and vision products transmit savings through different renewal and rate-setting mechanisms.
d5: Enrollment across product lines may double-count people and is not constant-quality service volume.
d5: Premium growth is dominated by medical-cost inflation, benefit, subsidy, and mix changes.
d5: Policy and reimbursement changes can sharply alter Medicaid, Medicare, and individual-market enrollment within the horizon.
o: Operator-required plan functions do not guarantee that an independent LMM carrier retains members or contracts.
o: Self-funded employers may use the same claims and network infrastructure while moving underwriting risk outside the direct-carrier lens.
o: Medicare, Medicaid, commercial group, individual, supplemental, dental, and vision plans have different substitution paths.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Direct Health and Medical Insurance Carriers

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Individuals, employers, associations, and government programs pay recurring premiums or capitation for the carrier to assume medical-cost risk, maintain networks and benefits, enroll and serve members, adjudicate claims, manage utilization, meet regulatory obligations, and pay covered providers. Premium and reimbursement rates are renewed or reset periodically, medical claims consume most revenue, and some products operate under federal or state program rules and risk adjustment.
lens.excluded_activities: HMOs that also provide health care, self-insured employer funds, health and welfare funds, reinsurers, brokers and agents without underwriting risk, third-party administrators without direct carrier risk, providers, software vendors, shells, subscale hobby businesses, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market U.S. direct carriers repeatedly underwriting health and medical insurance risk for external individuals, employers, associations, and government-program members, including risk-bearing group and individual plans, Medicare and Medicaid products, supplemental coverage, dental and vision coverage, and HMOs that provide insurance without directly providing health care.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS reports the May 2023 six-digit NAICS 524114 occupation and wage mix, including 32.43% office and administrative support, 22.60% business and financial operations, 13.02% computer and mathematical, 12.03% management, 5.84% sales, 16.46% customer-service representatives, 6.11% insurance claims and policy-processing clerks, and 4.43% claims adjusters, examiners, and investigators; NAIC identifies AI use in prior authorization, claims adjudication, data processing, pricing and plan design, risk adjustment, contracting, fraud, marketing, and risk management. Transformation: wage-weighted task exposure is assigned to document intake, enrollment, member service, provider data, claims and appeals support, coding and anomaly review, prior authorization, sales support, reporting, and portions of underwriting and analytics, after excluding work already automated and accountable clinical, actuarial, network, legal, compliance, and exception decisions. Direction of error: BLS directly matches the industry but not the LMM lens or task content; occupation titles can overstate exposure when work requires judgment and regulation, while rapidly improving language and document models may expose more current work.
caveats: The BLS table covers employers of all sizes and does not separate small risk-bearing carriers from large national plans.
Occupation shares are not task-level AI exposure and include work already performed by rules engines, portals, and electronic claims systems.
Commercial group, individual, Medicare, Medicaid, supplemental, dental, and vision products have different labor and clinical-review intensity.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit LMM time-and-payroll study separating enrollment, member service, claims, prior authorization, provider operations, actuarial, underwriting, sales, compliance, appeals, clinical review, IT, and already-automated hours would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Health carriers have large volumes of structured transactions, calls, forms, claims, authorizations, provider records, contracts, and regulatory submissions. AI can reduce handling time and avoid incremental hiring, but clinical and coverage judgment, appeals, network negotiation, actuarial signoff, model governance, consumer protection, and high-cost exceptions preserve substantial labor.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: NAIC reports total health enrollment rising 8.5% to approximately 295.5 million in 2025, with Medicare Advantage and group comprehensive enrollment growing while Medicaid declined, and direct written premium rising 15.2%; it attributes financial pressure to high utilization and medical-cost growth. Transformation: one-year enrollment and nominal premium movements across overlapping product lines are translated into a modest five-year constant-price, constant-quality insured-service ratio after excluding premium inflation, risk adjustment, benefit changes, subsidy shifts, duplicate enrollment, and product mix. Direction of error: aging and persistent medical need can increase plan demand, while public-policy changes, employer self-funding, affordability, Medicaid redeterminations, and subsidy changes can reduce direct-carrier covered lives; the reported aggregate may count a person in multiple coverages.
caveats: Enrollment across product lines may double-count people and is not constant-quality service volume.
Premium growth is dominated by medical-cost inflation, benefit, subsidy, and mix changes.
Policy and reimbursement changes can sharply alter Medicaid, Medicare, and individual-market enrollment within the horizon.
d5.change_evidence: CMS, NAIC, Census, and employer-benefit forecasts through 2031 for unique covered lives, member months, employer self-funding, Medicare Advantage, Medicaid, individual and group enrollment, benefit richness, and constant-price premium by product would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Recurring medical need, employer-sponsored coverage, Medicare growth, chronic disease, and consumer demand for financial protection support durable insured-service volume. Medicaid policy, affordability, employer self-insurance, exchange subsidies, benefit design, and government reimbursement can move enrollment materially, so nominal premium growth is not treated as real demand growth.

caveats: No source measures default-lens eligibility among the frozen LMM-band firms.
The 41-firm anchor uses a general-insurance margin bridge that may not fit health carriers with high medical-loss ratios, risk adjustment, and government-program revenue.
Legal entities, plans, insurance groups, regional subsidiaries, and provider-sponsored arrangements are not interchangeable transaction units.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level audit of the frozen 41-firm population using NAIC legal-entity filings, covered lives, product and geography mix, ownership, statutory capital, RBC, medical-loss and administrative ratios, network and government contracts, normalized earnings, licenses, and change-of-control rights would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition directly identifies external risk-bearing health insurers and expressly excludes provider HMOs and captive employee-benefit funds, so many firms fit the default recurring-service lens. Eligibility is reduced by runoff or restricted entities, subsidiaries that cannot transfer independently, plans dominated by one sponsor or government contract, entities without portable networks or licenses, carriers whose statutory capital or medical-loss experience blocks a transaction, and firms misclassified into the LMM EBITDA band by the general-insurance margin bridge.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 524114 ($47,985,814,684) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 524114 ($1,184,007,433,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2357 (margins.json[524]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Insurance (General) EBITDA/Sales 23.57%); revenue/employee $2,171,586 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 524114); class <5: 644 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,429,646 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $336,968 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 65 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,575,354 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $842,711 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,119,634 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,856,598 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 73 firms, avg revenue/firm $73,728,342 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,377,770 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 82 firms, avg revenue/firm $520,654,439 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $122,718,251 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 166 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,833,021,717 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,610,543,219 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 41

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: Census defines the industry by assuming health-insurance risk and assigning premiums; NAIC describes recurring premiums, plan payment of covered provider bills, networks, enrollment, and state oversight, while NAIC's AI guidance keeps carriers accountable for lawful and fair AI-supported decisions. Transformation: risk assumption, capital, licensing, benefit design, provider networks, enrollment, claims payment, appeals, and regulatory accountability are treated as operator-required, while self-insured employers, public programs, provider risk, third-party administration, and software-enabled self-service can remove quantity from the direct-carrier lens. Direction of error: regulated risk bearing makes software-only elimination unlikely, but continued employer self-funding, public-program changes, provider-sponsored risk, and consolidation into national platforms could remove more demand from current LMM direct carriers than assumed.
caveats: Operator-required plan functions do not guarantee that an independent LMM carrier retains members or contracts.
Self-funded employers may use the same claims and network infrastructure while moving underwriting risk outside the direct-carrier lens.
Medicare, Medicaid, commercial group, individual, supplemental, dental, and vision plans have different substitution paths.
o.change_evidence: Five-year CMS, ERISA, NAIC, and state data on fully insured versus self-funded enrollment, direct-contracting employers, provider-sponsored risk, public-plan migration, third-party administration, autonomous claims and authorization, carrier exits, and covered lives retained by LMM direct carriers would materially change this share.
o.rationale: AI can automate member interactions, data processing, claims support, authorizations, pricing analysis, and fraud detection, but a licensed or otherwise accountable organization must still bear medical risk, contract with providers, maintain capital, administer benefits, resolve appeals, pay claims, and comply with state and federal rules. Substitution is more likely to change who operates the plan than eliminate the function.

q.bridge: Source population and quantity: CMS requires many health issuers to spend at least 80% or 85% of premium on medical care and quality improvement and to rebate shortfalls; NAIC reports 2025 health-carrier net margin of 0.4%, a 90.3% aggregate loss ratio, a 10.3% administrative-expense ratio, annual premium and claims changes, and periodic premium resetting. Transformation: fixed premium during a coverage year allows initial retention of administrative savings, then the estimate discounts annual rate renewal, MLR rebates where applicable, government payment resets, employer and member competition, provider and broker sharing, medical-cost offsets, risk adjustment, software costs, and regulatory remediation over five years. Direction of error: lower administrative cost can improve profit without directly lowering the MLR numerator, so retention may be higher where the plan already clears the threshold; however thin observed margins, rate review, procurement, and intense renewal competition may pass benefit through faster than the base assumes.
caveats: Neither CMS nor NAIC measures five-year retention of implemented AI benefit at LMM carriers.
MLR rules differ by market, and administrative savings do not mechanically create a rebate if the claims-and-quality share remains above the threshold.
Commercial, Medicare, Medicaid, supplemental, dental, and vision products transmit savings through different renewal and rate-setting mechanisms.
q.change_evidence: LMM carrier cohorts showing administrative cost and medical-loss ratios, MLR rebates, renewal pricing, government rate resets, broker and provider payments, risk-adjustment transfers, AI and integration cost, service errors, and realized labor savings for five post-deployment years would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Carriers can initially retain lower service, claims-handling, and administrative cost because premiums are set in advance, but many products reprice annually and buyers compare total rates. Statutory MLR and rebate rules, government program rate setting, employer procurement, broker pressure, risk adjustment, provider contracting, utilization response, and AI vendor costs constrain durable capture.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: NAIC reports that 92% of 93 responding health insurers currently use, plan to use, or plan to explore AI or machine-learning models and lists use in contracting, prior authorization, fraud detection, product pricing and plan design, data processing, risk adjustment, marketing, risk management, and claims adjudication. Transformation: broad carrier-level adoption interest is converted into the share of exposed tasks an LMM carrier can operationalize within five years after discounting exploration, limited production coverage, legacy integration, data quality, third-party validation, cybersecurity, clinical governance, appeals, fairness, explainability, state and federal oversight, and change management. Direction of error: respondents may be larger and better resourced than LMM carriers and the 92% measure does not report transaction coverage or labor savings; vendor platforms and standardized claims data could nevertheless spread implementation quickly.
caveats: The 92% statistic combines current use with plans and exploration and is not an implementation or realized-savings rate.
NAIC states that AI generally supports rather than fully replaces underwriters, claims professionals, agents, and service representatives.
Prior authorization and claims models face especially high clinical, fairness, documentation, and appeal constraints.
rho.change_evidence: NAIC health-survey cuts or state and CMS examination cohorts for small and midsize carriers showing production model use, eligible transaction share, straight-through processing, human overrides, denial and appeal outcomes, error rates, complaints, implementation cost, and labor hours would materially change this range.
rho.rationale: Health-insurance AI has many production-relevant workflows and a high reported carrier adoption-or-interest rate. Implementation remains incomplete because errors can deny or delay care, trigger appeals and enforcement, create discrimination risk, and require human clinical review; smaller carriers also depend on legacy core systems and third parties with limited internal model-risk capacity.

s5.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; NAIC reports 1,156 health-statement filers in 2025 but does not identify a qualifying LMM control-transfer rate. Transformation: employer intent is sharply discounted for plans that do not close, gifts and internal transfers, subsidiary reorganizations, sponsor-controlled or nonprofit entities, government-contract dependence, network concentration, statutory capital, medical-cost liabilities, regulatory approval, and the absence of industry-specific completed-deal evidence. Direction of error: general employer intentions likely overstate transfers of regulated carriers, but consolidation demand for licenses, covered lives, networks, government-program capabilities, and local market access could raise transferability above this estimate.
caveats: Gallup measures cross-industry intentions rather than completed health-carrier transfers.
The NAIC filer count includes legal entities and organizational forms outside the LMM control lens.
No cited source supplies an industry denominator of completed qualifying control deals.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of NAIC and state Form A approvals, health-carrier merger filings, CMS plan novations, and commercial transaction data matched to NAICS 524114 LMM legal entities, excluding internal reorganizations and noncontrol plan or asset transfers, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: A qualifying health carrier can offer licenses, members, provider contracts, risk-adjustment capabilities, claims infrastructure, data, and local market access. Buyers must absorb volatile medical costs, capital needs, network obligations, rate and product approvals, government contracts, appeals and conduct exposure, cybersecurity risk, and change-of-control scrutiny, while many entities are nonprofit, provider-sponsored, affiliated, or not independently transferable.

principal_driver: Large, repetitive claims, service, authorization, enrollment, and data workflows create substantial scope for governed AI assistance and avoidable hiring.
principal_weakness: Medical claims consume most premium, margins and renewal pricing are constrained, and only a small uncertain population of independently transferable LMM risk-bearing carriers is available.
